emergent_themes.description: Participants cancelling subscriptions as a form of self-control rather than due to financial constraints - using friction as a behavioral management tool
Food content on social media platforms directly triggering ordering behavior, leading some participants to actively avoid such content
Traditional/cultural foods serving as stress relief and emotional regulation, contrasting with healthy Western food being perceived as stressful to prepare
One negative experience with a competitor platform creating lasting loyalty to preferred platform, even when price differences exist
Post-graduation shift from convenience-driven to health-driven food decisions, with delivery seen as obstacle to health goals rather than financial constraint

coding_summary: P008 represents a fascinating case of deliberate behavioral management post-graduation. Manoj demonstrates sophisticated self-awareness about his ordering triggers (social media, fatigue, notifications) and has implemented systematic restrictions (cancelled subscriptions, deleted TikTok, avoided food content) to support his health goals. His relationship with food is culturally nuanced - traditional Nepalese cooking provides stress relief while healthy Western meals feel stressful. A single negative experience with Deliveroo has created lasting loyalty to Uber Eats, showing how platform switching can be driven by isolated incidents rather than systematic comparison. His post-graduation transformation from heavy user to restricted user is health-motivated rather than financially driven, suggesting he may return to delivery apps once health goals are achieved.
coverage_gaps: RO3_emotional_social.is_there_stigma_around_solo_ordering_or_ - P008 lives alone and orders solo regularly without expressing stigma concerns

RO4_post_graduation.does_the_value_equation_for_delivery_shi - While P008 discusses habit changes post-graduation, he doesn't explicitly compare value perceptions between student and working life periods
